13: hinneh yaskil avdi yarum venissa vegavah meod (hinnēh yašĕkîl ʿabdî yārûm wĕniśśāʾ wĕgābahh mĕʾōd): 'Behold, my servant shall act wisely; he shall be high and lifted up and shall be exalted.' The fourth Servant Song (Isaiah 52:13-53:12) opens with the Servant's triumph — yarum venissa vegavah (high, lifted up, exalted) — three verbs used for YHWH's own majesty in Isaiah 6:1 and Isaiah 33:10. The Servant shares the divine exaltation-vocabulary. The shock of the Song: the path to this exaltation is through disfigurement so severe that people were appalled (Isaiah 52:14), and the means of exaltation is vicarious suffering (Isaiah 53:4-6). The Hebrew nasa (to bear/lift) runs through the Song: Isaiah 52:13 (be lifted up), Isaiah 53:4 (bore our griefs), Isaiah 53:11 (bear their iniquities), Isaiah 53:12 (bore the sin of many) — the lifting of the Servant and his bearing of sin are different sides of the same word.
7: noogesh vehu naaneh velo yiptach piv kesh latabach yuval vekrachel lifnei gozezeiha nelamah velo yiftach piv: 'He was oppressed and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth; like a lamb that is led to the slaughter, and like a sheep that before its shearers is silent, so he opened not his mouth.' The lamb-image combines the Passover lamb (Exod 12) and the Day of Atonement offering — an intentional fusion of Israel's two great atoning sacrifices. The silence contrasts with every protest of innocence in biblical lament-psalms: the Servant has no claim to make, no defense to offer — or rather, his silence is itself the defense, the refusal to protect himself from the death that will accomplish others' salvation. Peter applies this silence to Christ's non-retaliation (1 Peter 2:23) and Luke applies the lamb-image to the Ethiopian eunuch's question about Isaiah 53:7 (Acts 8:32-33).
3: YHWH grounds the promise of redemption with a paradox: you were sold for nothing, and without silver you will be redeemed. Because the exile was not a legitimate sale — Babylon received nothing of real value, only YHWH permitted the transfer for discipline — there is no ransom price to be paid to Babylon. YHWH redeems his people not by paying Babylon off but by exercising his sovereign authority. This will become the pattern of the costless-yet-costly redemption of the Servant (Isaiah 53:4-6).
4: Two paradigmatic oppressions: Egypt (voluntary sojourn, then slavery) and Assyria (forced conquest, for nothing). In both cases YHWH ultimately intervened. The point is that YHWH has precedent for liberating his people from great powers — Babylon is simply the latest instance.
5: YHWH asks what stake he has in the current situation. His people are taken for nothing, their rulers howl with contempt, and his name is blasphemed. The blasphemy of YHWH's name among the nations is the trigger for action. YHWH's honor and his people's redemption are bound together.
6: Therefore my people will know my name — the exodus pattern: YHWH reveals his name through his acts of deliverance (cf. Exodus 6:7). On that day they will know that I am the one who says, Hineni — Here I am. The self-disclosure formula is the same YHWH used at the burning bush and with the servant. Knowing the name means knowing the character revealed through act.
8: The watchmen of Zion raise their voices in united shout because they see eye to eye when YHWH returns to Zion. The watchman's job is to first sight the returning king; their unanimous rejoicing signals that the report is confirmed and unambiguous. The return of YHWH to Zion is the goal of the entire movement begun at Isaiah 40:1.
9: Even the ruins of Jerusalem are commanded to burst into singing — inanimate rubble participates in the restoration. The ground: YHWH has comforted (niham) his people and redeemed Jerusalem. Comfort and redemption are the double foundation that the whole of chapters 40-55 has been building toward.
10: YHWH has bared his holy arm — made it visible, unsheathed his power — before all nations. The qodesh/holy arm (holiness, disp 2): the manifestation of YHWH's saving power is simultaneously the revelation of his holiness. All ends of the earth will see YHWH's salvation. The universal scope means Israel's redemption is the vehicle for worldwide revelation of who YHWH is.
11: The call to depart Babylon: Leave! Touch nothing unclean! The priestly logic — those who carry YHWH's vessels must be purified — echoes the Levitical purity of the wilderness tabernacle. The exilic priests will lead the return in a processional that is simultaneously an act of worship. The departure is both political liberation and cultic consecration.
12: You will not leave in a hurry or flee in panic — contrast with the original Exodus departure, which was hasty (Exodus 12:11). The new exodus will be ordered, majestic, YHWH himself going before and also serving as rear guard. The rearguard imagery (cf. Isaiah 58:8) shows YHWH encircling his people with protective presence, not merely leading from the front.
14: The counterpoint to exaltation: many were appalled at him because his appearance was so disfigured beyond any human likeness. The disfigurement is described in the strongest possible terms — beyond human. This prepares the reader for the Servant's suffering (Isaiah 53:2-9) while holding the exaltation (v 13) in tension: the same figure who will be lifted up is the one so marred that people turn away.
15: So he will sprinkle many nations — the verb nazah (sprinkle) is priestly, used of blood-sprinkling for purification and atonement (Lev 4, 14, 16). Kings will be struck silent before him, seeing what they had not been told, understanding what they had not heard. The reversal: those appalled (v 14) become those who comprehend and are silenced before the Servant's exaltation. Paul echoes this in Romans 15:21 as the pattern of Gentile gospel reception.
1: Wake up, wake up! Clothe yourself with strength, O Zion! Put on your beautiful garments, Jerusalem, holy city! The parallel to Isaiah 51:9 (the exiles' cry for YHWH's arm to wake up) is now inverted: YHWH calls Zion to wake up and clothe herself in strength, just as his arm is clothing itself. The holiness vocabulary — qodesh (holiness, disp 2) city — designates Jerusalem as set apart for YHWH's purposes. The garments of strength and beauty replace the garments of mourning and dust (v 2). The uncircumcised and unclean will no longer enter: the restored city will be what the wilderness tabernacle was meant to be — a space ordered around YHWH's presence.
2: Shake off the dust, rise up, sit enthroned, Jerusalem! Loose yourself from the chains around your neck, captive daughter Zion! The seated-in-dust posture is the posture of a conquered city in mourning; sitting enthroned is the posture of a queen. The chains are the specific bonds of captivity in Babylon (cf. Isaiah 51:23 — the tormentors who walked over Jerusalem). The command to self-liberate is paradoxical: the release is YHWH's act (vv 3-6), yet Zion is called to rise as if she were already free — the posture of faith anticipating the coming reversal.
